quote: I'm moving I'm military, so I'm moving to Virginia. I'm you know, which I have I don't know what utility cost or anything are, but I have read articles how, like, the cost of electricity has skyrocketed, that kind of thing, in Virginia.
stewart_read: The customer volunteered his motivation unprompted: he has read about Virginia electricity costs and is already framing solar as a potential hedge. This is a bill-pain signal without a bill — the customer is pre-anchored on cost anxiety. Tyke acknowledged it briefly but did not name it back or anchor on it. The emotional alignment-against-utility was available here and was not deployed.
alternative_hypothesis: Tyke may have been holding the bill-pain anchor for the specialist, keeping setter scope clean. But the customer named the pain himself — reflecting it back costs nothing and costs no scope.
coaching_implication: Ask Tyke: 'When the customer told you he'd read about Virginia electricity costs skyrocketing, what did you do with that?' The customer handed him the emotional anchor. The move was to name it back — 'So you already know what's coming on that bill' — and let the customer feel heard before moving to qualify. The customer named a multi-year horizon and a cost anxiety. That's a qualifying asset worth anchoring. Ask Tyke what it means for the design conversation if the customer is there long enough to see real savings.

quote: And then, is there any good is there gonna be anybody else with title with you when you close on Friday?
stewart_read: Tyke asked the title question correctly and confirmed the wife is on the mortgage and title. He then did not include her in the appointment. Per protocols.spouse_decision, the correct move is to schedule the appointment for a time when both decision-makers can be present. Tyke captured the data and did not act on it.
alternative_hypothesis: The customer is the initiator and appears to be the primary decision-maker on this inquiry. The wife's exclusion may be lower-risk than in SESSION20's Andy scenario given the customer's self-motivated profile — but the protocol gap is real regardless of risk level.
coaching_implication: Show Tyke the codex line: 'If spouse on title, they MUST be on the appointment.' Then ask: 'You confirmed the wife is on title. What happened next?' The fix is one sentence at scheduling: 'Is there a time that works for both of you to be on that call?' The appointment is not structurally broken — the customer is genuinely motivated — but the wife's absence is an unresolved variable the closer will have to manage.

quote: in order to qualify for all the current programs going on right now excuse me. All we ask of the homeowner is that you just have a credit score of at least 670. Is that gonna be an issue for you at all?
stewart_read: Tyke stated the credit threshold at 670 — the correct schema value. This is a positive data point given the documented drift in SESSION18_fcbf33c1 where the rep stated 650. Tyke executed the qualifier at the right number, in the right place in the call sequence, without making it feel like an interrogation.
coaching_implication: Use this as a positive reinforcement moment with Tyke: 'You hit the credit qualifier at the right number and in the right tone — the customer didn't even flinch.' Then use it as a floor coaching reference: SESSION18_fcbf33c1 stated 650; Tyke stated 670. The manager should confirm which number is current policy and use this call as the positive exemplar for correct execution.

quote: Yeah. We can definitely just shoot it for next week then. Again, that's just a preliminary call to make sure all the numbers make sense and just kinda give you a full idea of what it would look like for this home.
stewart_read: The strategic read holds here: 'preliminary' was deliberate stakes-lowering appropriate to a pre-occupancy customer who had just given three consecutive scheduling deflections. Tyke read the customer's situation correctly — he is not yet in the home, has no bill, and is making a decision with incomplete information — and framed the appointment as low-commitment to keep the booking alive. This was the right call for this customer at this moment.
alternative_hypothesis: The reactive read would be that 'preliminary' undersells the appointment and reduces the customer's perceived obligation to show. That risk is real — but given the three deflections and the pre-occupancy context, a harder close on appointment value would likely have lost the booking entirely.
coaching_implication: Affirm the strategic instinct with Tyke, then offer a refinement: 'preliminary call' can be replaced with 'your personalized design review' — same low-pressure intent, but it names something the customer owns rather than something that sounds like a formality. The goal is to keep the stakes low while making the appointment feel worth protecting.

quote: We can do anytime between, 10AM and 7PM.
stewart_read: Tyke confirmed the one-hour block and the no-driving requirement, but the button-up was missing the 'is that fair?' verbal handshake and the explicit phone-appointment specification. The customer confirmed 10AM works, but there was no closing handshake to lock the commitment. On a self-motivated customer this is lower-risk than on a cold lead, but the protocol exists precisely because the handshake is predictive of show rate.
coaching_implication: Pull up the button_up checklist with Tyke and walk through what he hit and what he missed. He got the time block and the no-driving anchor. He missed 'is that fair?' and the explicit phone-appointment call-out. One sentence fixes both: 'So we've got you Wednesday at 10, one hour, phone call, not driving — is that fair?' Ask Tyke to add that to his close on every call.

quote: Alright. And then one last question, sir. So, like, whoever's gonna be calling me, is it gonna be the same area code? It's just because, like, I get so much so many, like, random ass phone calls, you know, that I don't answer.
stewart_read: The customer's area-code question is a trust signal, not a logistical one. He is telling Tyke: 'I want to show up for this appointment but I need help protecting it from my own spam-call filter.' Tyke's response — promising to include the number in the reminder text — was a genuine micro-trade: a personal commitment to solve a specific anxiety. The customer accepted it warmly and closed the call positively.
alternative_hypothesis: One could read this as a soft exit — the customer manufacturing a reason not to answer. But the customer's tone throughout the call was cooperative and self-motivated, and his explicit request for the number suggests he wants to answer, not avoid.
coaching_implication: Use this moment to show Tyke what a micro-trade looks like when it works. The customer handed him a specific anxiety; Tyke made a specific commitment. The coaching refinement: make sure the reminder text actually includes the specialist's number. If it doesn't, the micro-trade becomes a broken promise and the appointment is at risk. Flag for the closer: confirm the callback number is in the Tuesday reminder text.

quote: is it an opportunity for me to, you know, invest in solar since we're gonna be there for a couple of years and, you know, hopefully, save some money?
stewart_read: The customer named a multi-year tenure horizon unprompted — 'a couple of years' — and framed solar as a savings investment. This is a qualifying asset: a customer who plans to stay long enough to see ROI is a stronger lead than one who is uncertain about tenure. Tyke did not name this back or anchor on it. The emotional alignment opportunity — 'you're going to be there long enough to see real savings' — was available and unused.
alternative_hypothesis: Tyke may have been moving efficiently toward qualification and left the emotional anchor for the specialist. But reflecting the customer's own framing back costs no scope and no time.
coaching_implication: Ask Tyke: 'The customer told you he's planning to be there for a couple of years and wants to save money. What did you do with that?' The customer named the customer's own motivation. The move was to name it back and let it land before moving to qualify. This is the bill-anchor depth gap — the customer handed Tyke the emotional hook and Tyke moved past it.

quote: One one last thing. Sorry. So in that next phone call that that I get, that's when I'm gonna be given, like, an estimate on, like, how much it would cost kinda thing.
stewart_read: The customer's closing question reveals he is not entirely clear on what the specialist call will cover — he is asking for confirmation that pricing will be discussed. Tyke answered correctly and held setter scope: 'my job is, again, purely to get all the information right and so that we can go and accurately design it.' This is a clean scope-hold under a direct question about pricing.
coaching_implication: Use this as a positive scope-discipline example with Tyke. He was asked directly about pricing and did not quote numbers or ranges — he redirected to the design and the specialist. The coaching note is minor: the answer could have created slightly more curiosity ('the specialist will show you exactly what it looks like for your specific home and your specific bill') rather than just explaining the process. But the scope hold was clean.
